title: The Quad-$C_5$ Graph: Maximum Contextuality Gap on Eight Vertices
abstract: We perform an exhaustive semidefinite-programming search over all 11{,}117 connected non-isomorphic simple graphs on eight vertices to maximize the quantum contextuality gap $Δ(G)=\vartheta(G)-α(G)$, where $\vartheta(G)$ is the Lovász theta function and $α(G)$ is the independence number of the exclusion graph $G$ within the Cabello--Severini--Winter framework for projective measurements. A previously uncharacterized graph on $n=8$ vertices and $m=10$ edges, which we name the Quad-$C_5$ graph (graph6 code: \texttt{GCQb`o}), achieves $Δ=0.46784$, surpassing the Wagner graph $W$ ($Δ\approx0.414$, $m=12$) with two fewer edges. We determine numerically, via the PSLQ integer-relation algorithm at 50-digit precision, that Quad-$C_5$ is a \emph{qutrit} contextuality witness with $η_3=1+\sqrt{5}$ (minimal polynomial $x^2-2x-4=0$), while numerical evidence indicates the Wagner graph requires a four-dimensional (two-qubit) Hilbert space. The graph contains four mutually overlapping induced five-cycles, and its adjacency spectrum is dominated by golden-ratio eigenvalues, tracing the contextuality advantage algebraically to the KCBS pentagon. Under depolarizing noise, Quad-$C_5$ at $d=3$ shares the critical visibility $v^*=1/(3\sqrt{5}-5)\approx0.585$ of the KCBS witness -- an analytically provable coincidence arising from a uniform shift of the graph parameters -- while at $d=4$ it strictly surpasses the Wagner graph in noise robustness.
summary: The researchers discovered a new 8-vertex graph, named Quad-C5, that provides the largest known quantum contextuality gap for this size. This graph is more efficient than previous benchmarks and shows enhanced robustness to depolarizing noise in higher dimensions. The discovery reveals a deep algebraic connection between this new graph and the well-known KCBS pentagon via the golden ratio.
main_problem: The paper aims to identify the graph on eight vertices that maximizes the quantum contextuality gap within the Cabello-Severini-Winter framework.
main_result: The discovery of the Quad-C5 graph, which achieves a maximum contextuality gap of 0.46784, surpassing the previously known Wagner graph while using fewer edges and providing superior noise robustness at d=4.
method: An exhaustive semidefinite programming (SDP) search over all 11,117 connected non-isomorphic graphs on eight vertices, supplemented by high-precision numerical refinement and the PSLQ integer-relation algorithm.
model_or_system: The study uses the Cabello-Severini-Winter (CSW) framework for projective measurements, modeling contextuality via exclusion graphs where edges represent orthogonal projectors in qutrit (d=3) and four-dimensional (d=4) Hilbert spaces.
key_observables: The contextuality gap (Delta), the Lovasz theta function (quantum bound), the independence number (classical bound), and the critical visibility (v*) under depolarizing noise.
important_parameters: Number of vertices (n=8), dimension (d=3, 4), edge count (m), and the visibility parameter (v) for the depolarizing channel.
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes the validity of the CSW framework for projective measurements and uses a standard depolarizing noise model.
figures_summary: Figure 1 provides a structural comparison between the newly discovered Quad-C5 graph and the Wagner graph.
paper_structure: The paper begins with an exhaustive search and identification of the optimal graph, followed by an algebraic characterization of its properties, a comparison of its efficiency and noise robustness against known witnesses, and a spectral analysis linking it to the KCBS pentagon.
why_interesting: This is interesting for quantum information theorists as it identifies a more efficient and robust resource for certifying quantum contextuality, which is a fundamental requirement for certain quantum computing protocols and device-independent tasks.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (a) The optimal witness Quad-C5(∆= 0.4678, 10 edges). Dark (hub) nodes have degree 3; light (leaf) nodes have degree 2. The four hub nodes {2, 3, 6, 7} form a K2,2 core; leaves attach via two pendant paths 3−0−5−2 (left col- umn) and 6−1−4−7 (right column). (b) The Wagner graph W (∆= 0.4142, 12 edges, 3-regular); dashed lines are the four “long diagonals” of the C8 cycle. Quad-C5surpasses W by 13% in contextuality gap with two fewer edges.
